one_line_takeaway: Prompting an off-the-shelf LLM to regenerate a clean version of an adversarially perturbed sentence recovers most of a frozen text classifier's accuracy, decisively beating prior masked-LM purification under strong (K=50) TextFooler attacks, though the flagship prompt assumes access to the true label.
key_finding: LLM-guided purification is especially effective against the stronger attack (K=50), where it far exceeds all baselines. On IMDb K=50 it recovers accuracy to 73.52 (BERT) / 76.16 (RoBERTa) vs 51.0 / 54.3 for the prior text-purification baseline [18] and 2.8 for the undefended BERT. On AG News it dominates at both settings, e.g. BERT recovers to 83.58 (K=12) and 81.3 (K=50) vs 61.5 / 34.9 for [18] and 32.8 / 19.4 undefended. The authors summarize an average post-attack accuracy improvement of over 65% versus the undefended classifier, and over ~20-25% over the best prior defense in most cases. Caveat visible in the paper's own Table 1: against the weaker K=12 attack on IMDb, the prior baseline [18] slightly outperforms the proposed method (81.5/84.2 vs 79.34/78.9), so the advantage is concentrated at the strong-attack regime. The ablation (Table 2, AG News) shows the full prompt P0 (81.3) beats label-free P1 (78, ~4% drop) and greatly beats plain paraphrase P2 (52.7), indicating the task-framing prompt, not mere paraphrasing, drives the gains.
core_contribution: The paper proposes an LLM-guided adversarial text purification defense: instead of explicitly characterizing discrete word-level perturbations, it prompts an off-the-shelf instruction-tuned LLM (GPT-3.5) to directly regenerate a clean, semantically-similar version of an adversarially perturbed sentence that the (unmodified, pre-trained) classifier will then classify correctly. The authors position this as the first use of the contextual-understanding and generation capabilities of LLMs for text adversarial purification, contrasting it with the prior sole text-purification baseline (Li et al. [18]), which greedily masks/reconstructs tokens with a masked language model. The defense is test-time only and requires no adversarial training or fine-tuning of the classifier.
threat_model: Defender protects a fixed, pre-trained text classifier (fine-tuned BERT or RoBERTa) with no knowledge of the attack type and no retraining. The adversary uses TextFooler, a strong black-box word-substitution attack, to perturb correctly-classified inputs into misclassifications, with candidate-list size K in {12, 50} (larger K = stronger attack). The purification module sits between the (attacked) input and the classifier and edits the text before classification. Notably, the primary prompt P0 supplies the LLM with the ground-truth correct label and the misclassified label, so the defense as designed assumes access to the true label at purification time; the P1 ablation removes the true-label instruction to test the label-free setting.
limitations: Evaluation covers a single attack (TextFooler), two datasets, and one closed-source LLM (GPT-3.5), so generality across attacks (e.g., BERT-Attack, character/sentence-level) and models is untested. Semantic similarity between purified and original text is only shown qualitatively (Table 3 case study), with no quantitative similarity, fluency, or clean-accuracy-preservation metric; no variance/standard deviations or significance tests are reported. Most importantly, the headline prompt P0 leaks the ground-truth label to the purifier, which is unrealistic for a deployed defense (an attacker-facing classifier does not know the true label); the more realistic label-free P1 is only reported for AG News. Reliance on a proprietary, versioned API raises reproducibility/cost concerns, and there is no adaptive-attacker evaluation (an adversary aware of the LLM purifier).

datasets_used: IMDb (binary sentiment classification of movie reviews: positive/negative)
AG News (4-way news topic classification: science, business, world, sports)
quantitative_results: IMDb, undefended fine-tuned BERT post-attack accuracy: 20.4 (K=12), 2.8 (K=50); clean 94.1
IMDb, prior Text purification [18] BERT: 81.5 (K=12), 51.0 (K=50); RoBERTa: 84.2 (K=12), 54.3 (K=50)
IMDb, Ours LLM-guided BERT: 79.34 (K=12), 73.52 (K=50), clean 94.54; RoBERTa: 78.9 (K=12), 76.16 (K=50), clean 95.06
AG News, undefended fine-tuned BERT post-attack: 32.8 (K=12), 19.4 (K=50); clean 92.0
AG News, prior Text purification [18] BERT: 61.5 (K=12), 34.9 (K=50)
AG News, Ours LLM-guided BERT: 83.58 (K=12), 81.3 (K=50), clean 95.12; RoBERTa: 82.84 (K=12), 81.4 (K=50), clean 94.76
Prompt ablation (AG News, BERT): full prompt P0 = 81.3, label-free P1 = 78, paraphrase-only P2 = 52.7 (clean 95.12)
Reported average post-attack accuracy improvement over undefended classifier: over 65%; improvement over prior text-purification defense: over 25% in most cases
baselines_compared: Text purification (BERT and RoBERTa) [18] - masked-LM greedy purification, the only prior text-purification defense
